behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Escalation quote: "discovers `.sql` files in a `migrations/` directory" + "applies them ... in version order". The failure is at a module handoff (file discovery/parsing/sort -> execution), so Unit is too low (MOCK-MISS). REAL-INFRA does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior; a local test DB + test directory is sufficient. Rejected System because no behavior text requires OS-level timing, subprocess semantics, or real network faults.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., `002_b.sql`, `001_a.sql`, `010_c.sql`), run the runner against a local test Postgres, and assert execution order from resulting schema/data plus `_migrations` rows is `001,002,010`.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Escalation quote: "records each applied migration in a `_migrations` metadata table" and "not re-executed on a subsequent run". This is persistence/state handoff across runs, not one pure function, so Unit is too low. No real OS/network/subprocess requirement in the behavior text, so not System. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run migrations once, capture a side-effect from a migration (table row/count or schema change), run again, and assert migration side-effects are not duplicated and `_migrations` has no duplicate applied entries.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Escalation quote: "fails, its changes are rolled back automatically" and "no further migrations ... are attempted". This requires transaction behavior + runner control flow across modules; Unit is too low. REAL-INFRA: a real local Postgres transaction is enough; no subprocess/OS/network-specific failure mode is required by the behavior text, so System is rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Use migrations where one file has multiple statements and an intentional failing statement in the middle, followed by another migration file. Run runner and assert: partial changes from failed file are absent (rollback), later files were not applied, and `_migrations` does not mark failed/later files as applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Escalation quote: "two instances of the runner start at the same time" and "across both instances". This explicitly requires concurrent separate-process behavior and real DB locking/race conditions; mocked or single-process integration can hide real failures (MOCK-HIDE). Rejected Integration because the behavior text itself requires process-level concurrency semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes concurrently against the same Postgres database and identical migration set. Assert final schema/data is correct, each migration appears exactly once in `_migrations`, and no migration side-effect is duplicated.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Escalation quote: "first run against a fresh database" + "`_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied". This is initialization ordering across DB setup and migration execution modules, so Unit is too low. No requirement for subprocess/OS/network anomalies, so System is rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Start with an empty test Postgres DB and a migration that depends on metadata tracking. Run once and assert `_migrations` exists and is initialized before first migration is recorded/applied (verified via observable DB state and applied records).
